summary:
The Tokugawa Shogunate (1603–1868 AD) was the last and most durable of Japan's feudal military governments, established by [[event:3308|Tokugawa Ieyasu]] after his victory at the [[event:2421|Battle of Sekigahara]] (1600) and his appointment as Shogun in 1603, inaugurating over 250 years of peace, internal stability, and controlled isolation that profoundly shaped Japanese society, culture, and identity. The Tokugawa system imposed a rigid social hierarchy (shi-no-ko-sho: samurai, farmers, artisans, merchants) and a political structure (the sankin-kōtai alternate attendance system) that required feudal lords (daimyo) to spend alternate years in Edo (Tokyo) — keeping them under surveillance, draining their resources, and preventing rebellion. The [[event:2418|Sakoku isolation policy]] (from the 1630s) expelled European missionaries, banned Christianity, restricted foreign trade to a few licensed ports (Nagasaki, Hirado), and prohibited Japanese from traveling abroad.

The period of the Edo period (as the Tokugawa era is also called) saw Japan develop a sophisticated urban commercial culture despite its nominal agrarian ideology. The merchant class (theoretically lowest in the social hierarchy) accumulated wealth that patronized a brilliant popular culture: [[event:3309|kabuki theater]], [[event:3310|ukiyo-e woodblock prints]], haiku poetry (Bashō), and the prose fiction of Ihara Saikaku. Edo itself became one of the world's largest cities by the 18th century, with a population exceeding one million. The [[event:2417|Shimabara Rebellion]] (1637–1638), a Christian-led peasant revolt crushed with great brutality, intensified the anti-Christian policies. The Tokugawa peace ended when Commodore Perry's 'Black Ships' arrived in 1853, forcing Japan to open to Western trade and initiating the political crisis that led to the Meiji Restoration of 1868.
